verdicts.evidence: The claim is narrowly scoped to CFAA liability and is accurate. (1) Quote support: The Morgan Lewis article explicitly states the CFAA's "without authorization" provision does not apply to public websites like LinkedIn, that violating a public site's user agreement alone is insufficient to trigger CFAA liability, and characterizes the ruling as making the Ninth Circuit "the most pro-data scraping circuit in the country." All three sub-assertions are directly supported by the cited source. (2) Independent corroboration: EFF (Apr 2022) and Wikipedia confirm the Ninth Circuit held scraping public data "likely does not" violate the CFAA because a public site "has erected no gates to lift or lower," consistent with the Supreme Court's "gates-up-or-down" framework in Van Buren v. United States (2021). This is current law as of the 2022 ruling and has not been overturned. (3) Source quality: The legal holding is corroborated by primary-adjacent and authoritative secondary sources (court opinion summaries, EFF). IMPORTANT QUALIFICATION (not a refutation of the claim as stated): The claim is correct that ToS violation alone does not trigger CFAA liability, but it should NOT be read as "scraping public sites is legally safe." In the Nov 2022 N.D. Cal. ruling, the district court found hiQ DID breach LinkedIn's User Agreement (breach of contract), and the case ended in a confidential settlement with a stipulated judgment under California tort theories (trespass to chattels, misappropriation). So ToS violations remain a separate, viable basis for contract/tort liability even where CFAA does not apply. The claim itself, being limited to CFAA, is not contradicted by this. Minor soft spot: "most pro-scraping circuit" is the article's editorial framing, not a court holding, but the claim attributes it to the article. Note the hiQ CFAA holding arose from a preliminary injunction posture ("likely" violates).
verdicts.counterSource: https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2022/04/scraping-public-websites-still-isnt-crime-court-appeals-declares (corroborates); https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HiQ_Labs_v._LinkedIn (notes Nov 2022 district court found hiQ breached LinkedIn's User Agreement, qualifying but not refuting the CFAA-specific claim)

Wikipedia (HiQ Labs v. LinkedIn) independently corroborates that hiQ prevailed on the CFAA/public-data question, applying the Supreme Court's Van Buren v. verdicts.evidence: The claim is accurately supported by both the cited source and the underlying primary ruling. The Morgan Lewis blog (Dec 2022) reports the Nov 2022 U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California decision in hiQ Labs v. LinkedIn, which held that hiQ had breached LinkedIn's User Agreement and that "provisions of a website user agreement that prohibit data scraping and creation of fake profiles are enforceable under a breach of contract claim." This is the actual holding, quoted accurately, not an overreach. The framing "even where CFAA does not apply" is also correct and is in fact the central lesson of the hiQ saga: the Ninth Circuit twice ruled (including after Van Buren remand) that scraping public data did NOT violate the CFAA, yet LinkedIn still prevailed on the separate breach-of-contract theory. So the two legal threads ran in opposite directions, exactly as the claim describes. Caveats that do NOT rise to refutation: (1) this was a district-court ruling, not binding appellate precedent, and the case settled, so it lacks formal precedential weight; (2) enforceability of any such contract clause still depends on valid contract formation/assent (clickwrap vs. browsewrap notice issues) — hiQ had affirmatively agreed to LinkedIn's terms. These qualify the strength/scope but the claim is stated as a general principle that is well established in the case law and legal commentary. Source quality is adequate: a major law firm (Morgan Lewis) blog corroborated against the documented court ruling; the claim is not extraordinary and is consistent with mainstream legal consensus.
verdicts.counterSource: No credible source contradicts the core claim. These limit scope but do not refute that anti-scraping user-agreement terms are enforceable via breach of contract even where CFAA does not apply.

The claim mischaracterizes its own source and rests on weak, self-interested grounds. The IDFederation page (Oct 5, 2018) is an advocacy/opinion piece by Ron Berg, executive director of IIABA's Agents Council for Technology (ACT), written to promote the "SignOn Once" credential standard/product. The verbatim source text is: "agencies soon will see their costs to manage passwords skyrocketing to AS MUCH AS $1,000 to $3,000 per user per year." Three distortions: (1) "soon will see" = a PROJECTED/future cost, not a current recurring cost as the claim states; (2) "as much as" = a WORST-CASE upper bound, not a typical range; (3) the figure is for "password management" GENERALLY, not carrier-portal credentials specifically. No data source, study, or methodology is cited — it is a bare assertion in a piece marketing a competing solution. The figure is ~8 years old (2018) relative to the 2026 research date. Independent searches (Bing, DuckDuckGo) found NO corroborating or independently-derived sources; the number traces solely back to this single self-interested ACT/IDFederation claim. This is marketing fluff with no primary research backing, presented as fact while distorting the qualifier-laden original.
verdicts.counterSource: No independent corroboration found in Bing/DuckDuckGo searches; figure traces solely to the 2018 ACT/IDFederation advocacy piece itself, which cites no study or methodology.

verdicts.evidence: REFUTED on multiple grounds. (1) SOURCE IS VENDOR MARKETING: idfederation.org/why-carriers-win-with-signon-once/ is a promotional page for ID Federation's own product "SignOn Once," using persuasion language ("Will you be left behind?") and other unsourced ROI claims ("70% reduction in help desk calls"). The $1,000-$3,000 figure carries NO citation, study, or methodology. (2) UNSUPPORTED/HEDGED: full sentence is "agencies soon will see their costs to manage passwords skyrocketing to as much as $1,000 to $3,000 per user per year" — a speculative worst-case ("as much as," "skyrocketing"), not a measured cost. (3) CLAIM OVERREACHES THE QUOTE: the article refers to general password management (ALL of an employee's passwords), but the claim narrows it to "carrier-portal credential/password management"; the source does not isolate carrier logins. (4) OUTDATED: published October 5, 2018 (~8 years old), authored by ACT's Ron Berg, in a fast-moving identity/credential field. (5) CONTRADICTED BY CREDIBLE BENCHMARKS: independent analyst-derived figures are far lower — Forrester's widely cited $70 per password reset and Specops' derived ~$136 per end user per year. verdicts.counterSource: Forrester Research ($70 per password reset, widely cited via Forbes, HYPR, Security Boulevard); Specops Software derived ~$136 per end user per year; Gartner (~40% of help desk calls password-related). Versus the unsourced vendor marketing figure on idfederation.org (2018).

verdicts.evidence: The supporting quote is real but materially misread. On https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-exchange/, the full context is: "Migration to Auth0 enables a single login across all Ivans products, including Ivans Exchange, eliminating the need for multiple credentials and streamlining user access." This SSO eliminates credentials ACROSS IVANS' OWN PRODUCTS (Ivans Exchange, Bookroll, Download, Marketing Insights) — unified via Auth0. It does NOT address logging into multiple third-party CARRIER portals (Travelers, Chubb, etc.). Ivans Exchange itself is a portal for managing carrier/MGA download (data-exchange) connections, not a credential vault or cross-carrier SSO. The claim that this "indicates credential proliferation is a recognized pain point the industry tooling addresses" overreaches: the tooling addresses intra-Ivans credential consolidation, not the cross-carrier credential lifecycle problem DataBraid targets (40-50 distinct carrier portal logins, provisioning/deprovisioning, MFA-broken shared logins). The quote does not support the broader claim about the carrier-portal credential proliferation pain.
verdicts.counterSource: https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-exchange/ — full quote context: "single login across all Ivans products... eliminating the need for multiple credentials"

verdicts.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary source (the vendor's own product page) and is current. The for-carriers page (https://www.ivans.com/for-carriers/products/ivans-eservicing/) states eServicing "automates these workflows, ditching the need to respond to one-off requests" and that "your agents get your answers directly in their management systems," covering policy, billing, claims, and payment inquiries. The for-agents page (https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-eservicing/) independently corroborates: "One-Click Agency Inquiry" provides "immediate access to policy, billing and claims information in carrier policy admin systems," lets agents "make payments directly within your management system," and "stay in your management system the entire time" / "Instantly receive policy and other business information you need directly within your management system." The claim's wording maps accurately to this language and is not an overreach. Caveat (qualification, not refutation): it is a vendor marketing claim, so it describes intended/marketed functionality rather than independently verified outcomes; the underlying mechanism still relies on carrier-portal connections via SSO ("Authenticate your agency just once with your carrier's portal"). No credible contradicting source was found (search engine fetches failed to return results). Because this is a descriptive claim about what a product is marketed to do—not an extraordinary performance/benchmark claim—the vendor primary source is sufficient source quality, and the claim should be cited as a vendor description, not as verified efficacy.
verdicts.counterSource: No contradicting source found; Google Search fetch returned an error/no results, so independent third-party verification of efficacy is absent. Only caveat is that all corroboration comes from Ivans' own marketing pages.

verdicts.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborating evidence. idfederation.org states carriers make a "minimal IT investment in ID Federation" to become "carrier of choice for agents," and that for agents the process works "With no effort on your part" because "Your carrier will handle the real implementation work." The architecture confirms the distinction: ID Federation's "SignOn Once" is a federated-identity Trust Framework (Dec 2023) where carriers must "consume secure tokens from the identity provider" and provision "Separate IDs for every user." This is a federated/SSO model implemented carrier-side, NOT an agency-side credential vault that stores carrier passwords and automates/scrapes logins. Vertafore corroborates it has offered integration "through our partnership with SignOn Once by ID Federation" since 2017. The claim that coverage "depends on carrier adoption rather than agency-side credential storage/automation" is exactly correct for a standard requiring per-carrier IT implementation. The quote is not misread, the source is primary and current, and it is not contradicted. One mild caveat: the model is also delivered via solution providers/AMS vendors, but this does not contradict the core claim that carrier-side implementation is the gating factor. No credible source disputing the architecture was found.
verdicts.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Corroborating: idfederation.org Trust Framework (Dec 2023); Vertafore SignOn Once partnership (since 2017); carrier FAQ requirements ("consume secure tokens from identity provider," "separate IDs for every user").

verdicts.evidence: Claim is well-supported by primary and independent sources. PRIMARY: idfederation.org states SignOn Once lets users "use just one identity and one password for all of your insurance business logins" and describes ID Federation as "the nonprofit alliance of agencies, carriers, solution providers and partners." The /about page independently corroborates it is a "nonprofit group comprised of insurance carriers, solution providers, industry associations and agencies" that built SignOn Once as a "Trust Framework," and explicitly frames password management as the industry's "No. 1 pain point" — directly supporting the claim that the cross-carrier login problem is recognized industry-wide enough to warrant a shared standard. INDEPENDENT THIRD-PARTY CORROBORATION: Insurance Thought Leadership describes ID Federation as a nonprofit of carriers/solution providers/associations/agencies that built "SignOn Once - a Trust Framework"; ProgramBusiness covered its launch quoting a BB&T board member ("save hundreds of hours per day for everyone involved in the business of insurance"); Vertafore is a founding member/certified provider. The quote accurately supports the claim, source quality matches claim strength, and no contradicting evidence was found. MINOR CAVEATS (not refuting): (1) the framework serves the entire insurance service chain (carriers, solution providers, agencies), implemented carrier-side — agents benefit but it is slightly broader than "specifically for agents"; (2) adoption appears limited — Vertafore noted as the "only agency management system provider currently certified," suggesting modest real-world penetration. These qualify but do not refute the factual claim that the standard exists and was created to address the cross-carrier login problem.
verdicts.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. Closest qualification: search results indicate limited certified-vendor adoption (Vertafore as sole certified AMS provider at one point), but this does not contradict the existence/purpose of the standard.

verdicts.evidence: The verbatim quotes ARE on the Ivans pages, so the claim is literally supported by its source — but the source is pure vendor marketing copy and the claim overreaches by implying a general solution. (1) Source quality: the ONLY support is Ivans' own product pages (https://www.ivans.com/for-carriers/products/ivans-eservicing/ and the agent-facing /for-agents/products/ivans-eservicing/). No independent/third-party source corroborates that SSO actually works this way at scale; multiple web searches (Google, Bing) returned no substantive coverage of adoption or limitations. (2) Critical unstated qualifier: SSO is carrier-dependent. The carrier page is literally titled 'For Carriers' and addresses carriers ('let YOUR agents authenticate... with YOUR portal'); the agent page says 'Authenticate your agency just once with your CARRIER'S portal' and references 'carrier partners' systems.' SSO only functions for carriers that have implemented Ivans eServicing SSO — it does NOT eliminate password management across an agency's full portal set (e.g., the prospect's 40-50 active portals) unless every carrier participates, which is not established and is almost certainly false in practice. (3) The phrase 'eliminating the need to store or manage passwords externally' is a per-participating-carrier marketing promise, not a general capability — presenting it as a blanket solution to the cross-carrier credential lifecycle problem is an overstatement. This is a marketing claim sourced only to vendor copy, with a load-bearing qualifier (carrier adoption) omitted.
verdicts.counterSource: https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-eservicing/ — agent-facing page frames SSO as per-'carrier's portal' and connectivity to 'carrier partners' systems,' indicating coverage is limited to participating carriers, not universal.

verdicts.evidence: The claim is accurate and well-supported. Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374 (2021), was decided 6-3 (majority by Justice Barrett, joined by Breyer, Sotomayor, Kagan, Gorsuch, Kavanaugh; Thomas dissented joined by Roberts and Alito). The supporting quote from the Proskauer article is a direct quotation of Justice Barrett's majority opinion: the CFAA's "exceeds authorized access" provision "does not cover those who, like Van Buren, have improper motives for obtaining information that is otherwise available to them." Wikipedia's account of the case independently corroborates both the 6-3 vote and the "gates-up-or-down" holding that improper motive for accessing otherwise-authorized information does not violate the CFAA. The claim faithfully restates the quote with no overreach. Source quality is adequate: Proskauer is a reputable law firm publication quoting the primary opinion verbatim, and the holding is confirmed by independent secondary sourcing. Not outdated (the decision stands as of 2026) and not a marketing/cherry-picked claim. Note: the claim's relevance to DataBraid is contextual — Van Buren narrowed CFAA liability for misuse of authorized access, but did not address access via shared/automated credentials in violation of carrier ToS, which remains a distinct risk; however the claim itself is correctly stated.

Claim is fully supported by primary vendor sources. The agent-facing page (https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-eservicing/) states the "One-Click Agency Inquiry" capability lets agents "Get immediate access to policy, billing and claims information in carrier policy admin systems," that "You can even make payments directly within your management system," and that agents "stay in your management system the entire time" while pinging carrier systems "with just a click of a button," receiving "policy and other business information you need directly within your management system." The carrier-facing source confirms "Ivans eServicing automates these workflows" and "your agents get your answers directly in their management systems." This corroborates all four inquiry types (policy, billing, claims, payment) and the core mechanism (answers delivered into the management system instead of individual carrier-portal visits). The claim is a current product-capability description, not an extraordinary quantitative benchmark, so primary vendor documentation is adequate source quality. No contradicting source found. Caveat: it is marketing material describing designed capability; actual coverage depends on which carriers participate in eServicing, but the claim as worded is accurate.

The supporting quote does appear nearly verbatim on the primary source (https://www.ivans.com/for-carriers/products/ivans-eservicing/): "Improve security and reduce effort by letting your agents authenticate themselves just once with your portal. No more storing passwords, managing them externally or responding to help desk requests," plus "Via single sign-on, agents can get immediate access to policy, billing and claims information in your policy admin system." So the literal wording is supported. BUT the claim is refuted on two grounds: (1) MARKETING FLUFF / SOURCE QUALITY: This is a vendor product-marketing page (the "For Carriers" section), making an unqualified capability claim ("eliminating the need to store or manage passwords externally") with no independent or technical corroboration. WebFetch of the agent-facing Ivans page found NO description of eServicing as an SSO solution, and no mention of how it handles credentials — the SSO benefit is only asserted in carrier-facing marketing copy. (2) OVERREACH/MISLEADING SCOPE: The entire page is written in singular, carrier-first-person terms ("your portal," "your policy admin system"), confirming eServicing SSO is a per-carrier feature that each individual carrier must implement and configure on its own portal. It is NOT a cross-carrier aggregator. The claim's framing ("a carrier portal") obscures that this only eliminates external password storage for the specific carriers that have built/enabled eServicing SSO — a small subset of the hundreds of portals a broker uses (the research context cites a Brown & Brown leader managing 40-50 active portals out of hundreds of appointments). It does nothing for the broad cross-carrier credential lifecycle problem and would not let DataBraid's prospect retire external password management. An unqualified, strong capability statement sourced solely to vendor marketing = refuted per the rubric.
verdicts.counterSource: https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/ — agent-facing Ivans page describes eServicing only as "Obtain client account info from carriers with the click of a button," with no mention of SSO or eliminating external password storage, contradicting the broad framing of the carrier-marketing claim.

verdicts.evidence: Claim is fully accurate. Van Buren v. United States was decided June 3, 2021 by a 6-3 vote. The majority adopted the narrow reading of the CFAA's "exceeds authorized access" clause: a person violates it only by obtaining information from areas of a computer (files, folders, databases) that are off-limits to them, NOT by accessing information they are permitted to reach for an improper purpose. The Proskauer source's quote ("does not cover those who, like Van Buren, have improper motives for obtaining information that is otherwise available to them") matches the claim verbatim. Wikipedia independently confirms the 6-3 vote, 2021 date, and the holding (Van Buren was authorized to access license-plate data, so his improper motive did not make it a CFAA violation). The claim's wording, vote count, year, and substance are all corroborated by two independent sources. Note: this is also the foundational case behind the later hiQ v. LinkedIn resolution, relevant to the startup's scraping/automation risk question.

The claim is directly and accurately supported by the cited primary source. BPI (Bank Policy Institute) states verbatim that consumers may be unaware "their credentials or data could be further shared and/or used beyond their initial authorized access," and separately that "data aggregators can gain access to data attributes beyond those needed to provide the product or service requested." The paraphrase in the claim matches the quote without overreach. Source-bias check: BPI is a bank lobby group with an interest in disparaging data aggregators, but this particular statement is a general, well-established information-security principle rather than a contested empirical assertion. Independent corroboration found no dissent: Proofpoint notes OAuth/credential-based access "maintain[s] authorized access even if user credentials are reset"; Push Security and Goodwin Law treat over-scoped/shared access as a genuine risk requiring permission minimization and auditing; Fiskil contrasts credential sharing as the riskier method versus API-based access. No credible source disputes the underlying principle. The claim is current (security principle, not time-sensitive) and the source quality matches the claim's modest strength.
verdicts.counterSource: No contradicting source found. BPI is a bank-industry lobby group (potential anti-aggregator bias), but the specific security principle is independently corroborated by Proofpoint, Push Security, Goodwin Law, and Fiskil.

verdicts.evidence: Primary source idfederation.org directly supports the claim. Carrier-side implementation requiring carrier IT investment: "minimal IT investment in ID Federation" (carriers) and "Your carrier will handle the real implementation work." No agency-side burden: SignOn Once works "With no effort on your part" for agents. Carrier-adoption framing: "You can be the carrier of choice for agents" — coverage depends on which carriers join. The about page confirms a standards-based federated "Trust Framework" (Dec 2023, current): "the only solution that enables one identity to replace multiple IDs and passwords," that "meets standard specifications to accommodate implementation differences among carriers, solution providers and other business partners." This describes a federated identity standard implemented by relying parties (carriers), which by architecture does NOT store/replay agency-held passwords or scrape — so the "distinguishing it from a credential-vault/scraping approach" portion is a correct analytical inference, not an overreach. No contradicting third-party source found. Caveat: the specific supporting phrases ("carrier of choice," "no effort") are promotional marketing language, but they accurately describe the established federated-standard architecture, not a fabricated benchmark. Could not decode the Trust Framework PDF (garbled font encoding) to verify SAML specifically, but the federated-trust-framework + carrier-implementation model is sufficiently established by the org's own pages to support the claim as stated.
verdicts.counterSource: No contradicting source found; searches (Google/Bing) returned no third-party material disputing or qualifying the carrier-implemented federation model.

verdicts.evidence: The claim is accurate and well-supported on every element. (1) Vote count: Van Buren v. United States was decided 6-3 (Barrett writing for the majority, Thomas/Roberts/Alito dissenting) on June 3, 2021 — confirmed by Wikipedia's case summary independent of the Proskauer source. (2) The holding: the Court adopted the narrow "gates-up-or-down" reading of "exceeds authorized access," ruling a person exceeds access only when obtaining information from areas (files/folders/databases) off-limits to them. (3) The improper-motives language: the claim quotes the Court verbatim — the provision "does not cover those who, like Van Buren, have improper motives for obtaining information that is otherwise available to them." This is a direct quotation from the majority opinion, not an overreach. The claim is current (the holding still stands as of 2026; no superseding SCOTUS ruling), and a contemporaneous law-firm legal analysis (Proskauer) corroborated by Wikipedia is sufficient quality for this well-established legal fact. No contradicting source found.
verdicts.counterSource: None found — both the Proskauer article and Wikipedia corroborate the 6-3 vote, June 3 2021 date, and the verbatim "improper motives" quote from the majority opinion.

verdicts.evidence: The supporting quote is confirmed verbatim on the source page (https://bpi.com/data-aggregators-issue-summary/): "their credentials or data could be further shared and/or used beyond their initial authorized access." The page additionally states "through screen-scraping, data aggregators can gain access to data attributes beyond those needed to provide the product or service requested." The claim ("may be shared or used beyond the originally authorized scope") faithfully paraphrases this with appropriate hedging ("may"), so it is NOT an overreach. The assertion is a non-extraordinary, broadly-accepted security principle (sharing credentials with a third party expands the surface for misuse beyond the original purpose); no credible source disputes it. CAVEAT FOR CONTEXT: BPI (Bank Policy Institute) is a banking-industry advocacy group with a channel-conflict interest against fintech data aggregators, and this content stems from the US open-banking / CFPB Section 1033 policy debate, so it is not a neutral primary security source — it should be cited as industry advocacy, not independent research. However, because the claim itself is a modest, well-established truism rather than an extraordinary claim, the source strength is adequate to support it. The principle remains current as of 2026.

The quote is accurate vendor copy, but the CLAIM overreaches on scope. Two grounds for refutation:

1) MARKETING FLUFF / NO INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION: The supporting quote ("No more storing passwords, managing them externally or responding to help desk requests") is verbatim from Ivans' own carrier-facing marketing page (https://www.ivans.com/for-carriers/products/ivans-eservicing/, "For Carriers" section, addressing "your agents"/"your portal"). It is a sales benefit statement aimed at carriers, not an independently verified outcome. No third-party source corroborates the password-elimination outcome.

2) MATERIAL SCOPE OVERREACH: SSO is enabled carrier-by-carrier — each carrier must implement Ivans eServicing SSO on its own portal. The agent-facing page (https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-eservicing/) says "Authenticate your agency just once with your carrier's portal" and One-Click Agency Inquiry gives "immediate access to policy, billing and claims information in carrier policy admin systems" — but only for participating carriers. Ivans does NOT publish a participating-carrier count, and access is limited to carriers who have enabled it. So for a brokerage using 40-50 active portals, it does NOT "eliminate the need to store or manage passwords externally" across the portfolio; it only removes the password burden for the subset of carriers on Ivans eServicing. The claim states this as an unqualified, universal capability, which misrepresents the carrier-by-carrier reality and the residual long-tail of non-participating portals that still require stored credentials.

The core mechanic (SSO + in-AMS policy/billing/claims access for enabled carriers) is real and supported, but the claim's framing as a general elimination of external password management is an overreach repackaging vendor marketing.
verdicts.counterSource: https://www.ivans.com/for-agents/products/ivans-eservicing/ (agent-facing page confirms per-carrier authentication scope; no participating-carrier count published) vs. carrier-facing marketing source of the quote https://www.ivans.com/for-carriers/products/ivans-eservicing/

verdicts.evidence: The claim is directly supported by the verbatim quote on https://bpi.com/data-aggregators-issue-summary/: "their credentials or data could be further shared and/or used beyond their initial authorized access." The claim is essentially a hedged paraphrase ("may be shared or used beyond originally authorized scope"), so it is NOT an overreach. The source also reinforces it with "aggregators can gain access to data attributes beyond those needed" and warns of "unauthorized data access and potentially fraudulent activity." The underlying principle (storing/sharing credentials creates over-broad-access risk) is a widely accepted security tenet and was not contradicted by any source. CAVEATS that lower confidence: (1) BPI (Bank Policy Institute) is a bank-industry lobbying/advocacy group with a direct channel-conflict interest in restricting third-party data aggregators, so this is advocacy, not a neutral primary source. (2) The claim is framed as a hypothetical risk ("could be"/"may be") with no quantification or documented incident, not proven harm. (3) Domain mismatch: the source addresses fintech/banking data aggregators scraping bank accounts, NOT insurance carrier portals — the mechanism is analogous and transferable but the source never mentions insurance brokerage. The claim survives because it is hedged, well-matched to the quote, and consistent with accepted security principles; but it should be cited as a banking-aggregator-derived risk analogy, not as insurance-specific documented evidence.
verdicts.counterSource: Source is BPI (Bank Policy Institute), a bank-industry advocacy group with vested interest against data aggregators; claim is a hedged hypothetical risk, and the domain is banking/fintech rather than insurance carrier portals. No source contradicts the claim, but its strength rests on a biased lobbying source and analogical transfer.

verdicts.evidence: The BPI primary source (https://bpi.com/data-aggregators-issue-summary/) was fetched and verbatim confirms the supporting quote: "Consumers input their username and password into the FinTech app, and the data aggregator uses these credentials to access the consumer's accounts." This directly supports the claim's core mechanic — users hand credentials to a third party that stores and uses them to log in on the user's behalf. BPI (Bank Policy Institute) is a credible industry/policy source, and the screen-scraping/credential-sharing model is uncontroversial and widely documented; no contradicting source was found. The second half of the claim — that a broker credential vault would use "the same credential-sharing model" against carrier portals — is an analogy added by the claim author, NOT asserted by the source, but it is a structurally accurate parallel (both involve surrendering portal logins to a third party that stores them and authenticates on the user's behalf) rather than a factual overreach. One mild qualification: in finance this credential-sharing/screen-scraping model is increasingly being superseded by tokenized/API (open-banking) access, so the model is "legacy" in banking; however the claim only describes how screen-scraping works (still accurate and still in use/litigated), and the analogy holds precisely because carrier portals generally lack such APIs. Source quality matches claim strength and the claim is current.
verdicts.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. Mild qualification only: industry trend (CFPB 1033 / open banking, BPI's own advocacy) favors tokenized API access over credential-sharing screen-scraping, but this does not refute the description of how screen-scraping works.
